Mycelium-Based Breakthroughs: Exploring 
Commercialization, Research, and Next-Gen Possibilities
Nungnit Wattanavichean1 · Jakkapon Phanthuwongpakdee2 · Preeyaporn Koedrith2 · 
Pitak Laoratanakul3 · Boonchock Thaithatgoon4 · Sayanh Somrithipol5 · 
Papichaya Kwantong5 · Salilaporn Nuankaew5 · Umpawa Pinruan5 · 
Charuwan Chuaseeharonnachai5 · Nattawut Boonyuen5
Circular Economy and Sustainability (2025) 5:3211–3253
https://doi.org/10.1007/s43615-025-00539-x
Abstract
Mycelium-based composites (MBCs) and pure-mycelium materials (PMMs) represent 
groundbreaking advancements in materials engineering, offering environmentally sustain-
able alternatives aligned with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). These inno-
vative materials integrate mycelium, a natural biological adhesive, with lignocellulosic 
by-products, demonstrating a multidisciplinary approach that merges biology, engineering, 
and design to address critical environmental challenges. This comprehensive review ex-
plores recent advancements in MBC technology, encompassing commercial applications, 
ongoing research and development, scientific literature, mycological insights, patents, and 
intellectual property. Selected product samples illustrate the versatility and potential of 
MBCs. The pivotal role of mycelium in addressing current and future challenges in ma-
terials science is underscored. The review identifies eleven distinct application areas for 
MBCs and PMMs, including five commercialized sectors and six under active research 
and development. Furthermore, key directions for future studies are outlined, emphasizing 
the integration of MBCs and PMMs to enhance the sustainability of conventional materi-
als and drive the evolution of next-generation materials. Keywords  Mycelium · Environmental Sustainability · Mycelium-based Composites · 
Lignocellulosic by-products · Next-gen Materials · Bio-based Materials
Introduction
Amid escalating environmental and sustainability crises, global dependence on synthetic 
materials, particularly plastics, presents a critical challenge. Plastic production has surged 
from approximately 1.5 million tons per year in 1950 to an estimated 359 million tons by 
2018, significantly contributing to environmental degradation [1–3]. The production of synthetic materials is energy-intensive and generates emissions detri-
mental to both the environment and human health. Plastics pose a severe ecological threat 
1 3

Circular Economy and Sustainability (2025) 5:3211–3253
due to their prolonged degradation timelines and persistent environmental contamination 
[4–6]. The growing problem of fossil-fuel-based pollution and plastic waste has far-reaching 
consequences for biodiversity, energy efficiency, and economic sustainability, necessitating 
an urgent transition t

oward biodegradable alternatives that harness the inherent sustainabil-
ity of natural materials [7, 8]. In response to these challenges, mycelium-based composites (MBCs) and pure-myce-
lium materials (PMMs) have emerged as promising eco-friendly alternatives [9–12]. These 
materials are produced by combining fungal mycelia—with its natural binding proper-
ties—with lignocellulosic biomass derived from agricultural by-products such as sugarcane 
bagasse, spent coffee grounds, and rice husks [13–16]. Lignocellulosic by-products, rich in 
cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, serve as essential substrates, providing nutrients for 
mycelial growth while supporting the formation of dense, interwoven hyphal networks that 
contribute to the mechanical integrity of the final composite. The physicochemical proper-
ties of these substrates, including particle size, porosity, and surface roughness, directly 
influence mycelial colonization, binding efficiency, and the resulting mechanical properties, 
such as compressive strength, elasticity, and water absorption [14, 15]. MBCs fabricated by 
different lignocellulosic waste, for example hemp, flax, flax waste, soft wood, and wheat 
straw, showed different content of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin as well as different 
substrate particle size [13]. Therefore, it led to variety of density, compressive modulus, and 
shrinkage. While chopped flax resulted in the highest compressive modulus, a loose pine 
wood gave the lowest one. Moreover, pineapple fiber has been proved to improve more than 
2-fold of flexural strength for MBC from spent coffee ground [16]. Furthermore, recent advancements in substrate pre-treatment techniques, such as steam 
explosion, alkali treatment, and enzymatic hydrolysis, have been shown to enhance sub-
strate compatibility, improve bioavailability, and increase mycelial binding efficiency. By 
utilizing these agricultural residues, the production of MBCs and PMMs not only offers 
an eco-friendly alternative to synthetic materials but also facilitates a sustainable method 
for valorizing agricultural waste into materials with both low and high economic value 
[17–19]. This approach highlights the critical role of lignocellulosic by-products in shaping 
the properties and applications of mycelium-based materials, while contributing to circular 
economic systems and reducing environmental impact. Characterized by reduced cost and density, alongside key attributes such as biodegrad-
ability, lightweight composition, fire and water resistance, economic feasibility, local 
sourcing, rapid growth, and energy efficiency, MBCs present a versatile alternative to con-
ventional synthetic and construction materials [18, 20–22]. Their successful application in 
construction, packaging, and furniture production can help mitigate critical environmental 
issues, including plastic pollution and the excessive consumption of natural resources in 
construction [23–26]. This review aims to consolid

ate and critically assess current research and emerging 
concepts on utilizing agricultural waste and fungal species for the synthesis of MBCs and 
PMMs. Fungi play a crucial role in advancing sustainable material technologies, paving 
the way for innovations in materials science and industry [27–29]. We explore the impact 
of innovative designs and patent trends in mycelium-based materials (MBMs), as well as 
commercial products and prototypes, in facilitating the transition from a petroleum-based 
economy to a bio-based, circular, and green economy aligned with the Sustainable Develop-
ment Goals (SDGs) [30–34]. 1 3

MBC and PMM applications are categorized into two main groups: commercialized 
products and those in the research and development phase. Commercialized applications 
include construction materials, artistic and product design, 3D printing, furniture, packag-
ing, textiles, fabrics, paper-like materials, fashion, and applications in the food and beverage 
industry. Meanwhile, ongoing research focuses on environmental remediation, agriculture 
and horticulture, health and medical devices, amulets and religious artifacts, space and auto-
motive industries, electronic devices, and energy production. Concept and Development of mycelium-Based Products
Fungi exhibit a remarkable ability to convert organic matter into a wide range of valuable 
products, offering innovative solutions to pressing global challenges [35]. Fungal biotech-
nology plays a pivotal role in transitioning from a petroleum-dependent economy to a bio-
based circular economy, enabling the sustainable production of resilient resources across 
multiple sectors, including food, feed, chemicals, fuels, textiles, and materials for construc-
tion, automotive, transportation, and furniture industries [36–39]. Since the late 20th century, advancements in biotechnology and a growing emphasis 
on environmental sustainability have driven research into mycelium-based applications. These applications fall into two material categories: mycelium-based composites (MBCs) 
and pure-mycelium materials (PMMs) (Fig. 1A). MBCs are created by integrating myce-
lium with organic substrates. Early research focused on leveraging mycelium for composite 
materials by inoculating fungal spores into organic substrates. As illustrated in Fig. 1B, MBC production begins with selecting an agricultural waste 
substrate and fungal species. The substrate is sterilized to prevent contamination before 
culturing the fungus. The mycelium then digests the substrate and binds it into a cohesive 
solid mass through a process known as myceliation, yielding materials that are lightweight, 
strong, and naturally biodegradable [21, 40]. Following cultivation, post-processing is carried out to deactivate fungal growth and 
enhance the material’s properties. Various substrates, including rice bran, sawdust, and corn 
pericarp, have been explored in combinatio

n with mycelium to tailor material characteris-
tics [41, 42]. Beyond digestible substrates, non-digestible materials such as textile waste, 
sand, and clay have also been utilized. Given that the substrate constitutes 70–80 wt% of 
MBC, its properties are influenced not only by the fungal species but also by factors such as 
substrate composition, moisture content, temperature, nutrient availability, pH, and oxygen 
levels. The interaction between fungal species and substrate types yields diverse MBC prop-
erties, mimicking natural materials, foam-like substances, and polymers [43, 44]. Modern 
engineering applications of MBCs have significantly expanded, encompassing eco-friendly 
packaging solutions and construction materials such as bricks and insulation panels. Unlike MBCs, whose properties are highly dependent on substrate type, pure-mycelium 
materials (PMMs) are fabricated exclusively from mycelium fibers or biomass, typically 
forming a mat or layer [45] (Fig. 1A). As a result, PMM properties are influenced solely 
by the fungal species, with different species producing distinct mycelium arrangements 
and compositions. Mycelium naturally grows into a network-like microstructure, where 
fibers interweave at the microscale [46]. This micro-woven characteristic enhances PMM 
strength. The mycelium cell wall comprises polysaccharides, chitin/chitosan, and glucan 
1 3
3213

Circular Economy and Sustainability (2025) 5:3211–3253
biopolymers, with the ratio of chitin/chitosan to glucan dictating its mechanical properties 
[33]. Variations in fungal species and growth conditions influence PMM growth patterns and 
polysaccharide composition. Additionally, PMMs exhibit properties characteristic of both 
polymers and elastomers and can be chemically or physically modified for diverse applica-
tions. Notably, PMMs are utilized as leather alternatives and textiles, demonstrating their 
versatility and potential in sustainable fashion and beyond [47, 48]. Advancements in mycelium-based materials (MBMs) demonstrate their ability to replace 
polymers, elastomers, and natural materials, positioning them as next-generation alterna-
tives [18, 20, 49]. Mycelium technology continues to evolve, propelled by ongoing research 
and innovation. Scientists and engineers are exploring novel substrates, optimizing growth 
conditions, and refining fabrication techniques to enhance the mechanical properties and 
functionality of MBCs. This progression—from traditional applications to contemporary 
engineering solutions—underscores the transformative potential of mycelium, paving the 
way for sustainable, fungi-derived materials to play a pivotal role across various industries 
[50]. Fig. 1  Concept of MBMs, encompassing MBC and PMM (A). Diagram illustrating the production work-
flow of the myco-fabrication process, detailing each stage from the agricultural sector to the market (B). 1 3

In Conjunction With Global Development Goals
The concept of a Bio-Circular-Green (BCG) economy offers a transformative approach to 
addressing global challenges such as environmental degradation, resource scarcity, and cli-
mate change. Central to this model is the integration of biological processes and materials 
into the economic system to create sustainable, circular, and green solutions [51, 52]. Fungi play a vital role in this paradigm by providing innovative and environmentally 
friendly alternatives applicable across various industries, including construction, packag-
ing, and product design [53–55]. This approach underscores the commitment to sustain-
able innovation, aligning with the BCG economy’s focus on biological processes and green 
growth [56]. As shown in Fig. 2, myco-composites, as part of this initiative, directly contribute to sev-
eral SDGs, such as promoting sustainable industrialization (SDG 9), fostering eco-friendly 
urban development (SDG 11), encouraging responsible consumption (SDG 12), mitigating 
climate change (SDG 13), and supporting biodiversity (SDG 15) [30, 57, 58]. This integration embodies the essence of sustainability and highlights the significant 
potential of myco-composites in driving forward the BCG economy. The sustainable pro-
duction of mycelium biocomposites, which avoid harmful chemicals and processes, supports 
biodiversity preservation and promotes sustainable land use practices, thereby contributing 
to ecosystem health. Moreover, these initiatives align with Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) cri-
teria by reducing environmental impact, enhancing community well-being, and promoting 
responsible governance through sustainable practices. The integration of BCG principles, 
SDGs, and ESG frameworks within mycelium material technologies exemplifies a holistic 
Fig. 2  Diagram depicting the potential applications of fungi in biotechnology and their alignment with 
SDGs, specifically SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure), SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and 
Communities), SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production), SDG 13 (Climate Action), and SDG 
15 (Life on Land)

sustainability model, demonstrating how bio-based innovations foster environmental stew-
ardship, social responsibility, and ethical governance, thereby contributing to a more sus-
tainable and equitable world [31, 59]. The utilization of fungi for myco-composites underscores the interdisciplinary nature of 
the BCG economy, effectively bridging biology, materials science, and environmental engi-
neering. This cross-disciplinary approach is essential for developing innovative solutions to 
address the complex challenges of sustainability [18, 60]. Academic and Investment Sectors
A literature survey of mycelium-based materials was conducted in June 2024 with Google 
Scholar using the keywords: “mycelium-based”, “mycelium materials”, “fungal materi-
al

s”, “mushroom materials”, “mushroom composite”, “mycelium composite”, “myce-
lium brick”, “mycelium leather”, “mycelium packaging”, “mycelium foam”, “mycelium 
mat”, “mycelium sheet”, “mycelium insulation”, “mycelium textile”, “mycelium absor-
bent”, “mycelium design”, “mycelium construction”, “mycelium furniture”, and “myce-
lium meat”. Patent analysis was conducted using Patent Analytics by Patsnap with similar 
keywords. The search period spanned from 2011 to 2024, and all results were manually 
screened for relevance to mycelium-based materials. The analysis revealed a significant rise 
in published articles on mycelium-based materials over the last decade, particularly from 
2016 to 2023, where the number of publications increased tenfold (Fig. 3A). Publications on 
MBMs have recently outpaced those on other fungal products like enzymes, antibiotics, and 
active compounds. By 2023, more than half of the publications in our literature survey were 
related to MBM. Ecovative filed a key patent for producing materials and composites using 
filamentous fungi growth in 2007 (IP no. US9485917B2). This demonstrated the commer-
cial viability of mycelium-based materials, leading to further patents from companies like 
Mycoworks Inc. and Bolt Threads for mycelium leather manufacturing. Major contributors 
to the patent landscape include Mycoworks Inc. (60 patents), Bolt Threads (49 patents), 
Ecovative Design LLC (25 patents), and Biohm Ltd. (10 patents) (Fig. 3B). Notable patent 
holders include Philip Ross (40 patents), Nicholas Wenner (33 patents), and Jordan Chase

(30 patents). Recent patents predominantly focus on cultivation methods, mechanical prop-
erty enhancement, and the development of specific mycelium-based products [61]. The patent landscape analysis in Fig. 3B illustrates the range of applications patented by 
various companies, divided into eight sectors. Most of these applications focus on extracting 
and cultivating mycelium for its active ingredients and enzymes, primarily used in the food 
and pharmaceutical industries. MBMs are targeted at five key applications: construction, com-
posites, packaging, pure mushroom sheets, and food applications [49]. For construction mate-
rials, MBCs have been developed for building panels, sandwich structures, and architectural 
models. These composites are valued for their aesthetic qualities and require enhancements in 
mechanical properties. In packaging, MBC is prized for its lightweight and degradable char-
acteristics. PMMs are mainly fabricated as sheets and foams, with significant potential in food 
applications as meat alternatives. Various cultivation techniques for mycelium have also been 
patented [53]. The patent landscape reveals substantial opportunities for further development 
of mycelium-base

d materials, which will be explored in the subsequent section of this review. Current Applications and Potential Ideas of MBCs and their Application
This section offers a comprehensive overview of the current applications of MBCs, catego-
rized into two primary groups. The first group consists of applications with commercialized 
products that are already available on the market (Sect.

4.1). The second group encompasses 
applications still under development (Sect. 4.2). Mycelium-Based Materials Applications With Commercialized Products
The utilization of mycelium as a foundational material in emerging biomaterials represents 
a rapidly expanding sector within materials innovation. Companies worldwide are leverag-
ing mycelium to develop sustainable alternatives to conventional products. Notably, Ecova-
tive, MycoWorks, and Mogu have explored mycelium’s unique properties to create diverse 
biomaterials, as outlined in Table 1. The development and application of MBMs indicate a broader shift towards sustainabil-
ity in material science. Ongoing research and development efforts are focused on enhanc-
ing the performance, scalability, and range of applications of MBM, promising further to 
cement their role in the future of sustainable materials [12, 13, 27]. MBMs and their prod-
ucts are used in various innovative ways across different industries, reflecting the material’s 
versatility and sustainability [62]. Notable examples listed in Table 1 are described below. Construction, Building Materials/ Infrastructure and Urban Planning/ Architecture 
Form
Mycelium bricks (myco-bricks) offer a sustainable alternative to traditional construction 
materials, as evidenced by their Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) [64–66]. Projects like “Hy-
Fi” have demonstrated the potential of these materials [67]. Over the past decade, numer-
ous large pavilion projects have highlighted the utility of myco-materials in construction, 
necessitating a distinction between their use in structural roles and as cladding or surface 
applications. For example, the “Shell Mycelium” pavilion in India [68], the “Living Pavil-

Sweden [72] and Blast Studio in London, UK [73], have developed unit-based columnar 
structures utilizing mycelium. Currently, Ecovative has integrated mycelium-based panels as insulation in a small-scale 
housing prototype, demonstrating a practical application of mycelium in the construction 
industry. These projects underscore the application of myco-materials at a building scale, 
primarily in non-structural capacities. Mycocrete is a construction material that includes mycelium. When infused into a fabric 
framework created through knitting, it generates a composite material that surpasses previ-
ous fungi-derived biomaterials in terms of durability and flexibility. This advancement may 
lead to the creation of buildings that are not only lighter but also have a minimal environ-
mental footprint [74]. As shown in Fig. 4, Mogu Acoustic panels and floor tiles represent the first commercially 
available products of their kind composed entirely of fungal mycelium and recycled textile 
residues [75]. In Poland, Fumo panels [76] offer an innovative interior finishing solution, 
crafted from bio-based and environmentally friendly mycelium-based materials, and are 
biodegradable, thus posing no negative impact on the natural environment. In Portugal, 
MykoFoam is an insulation panel composed of renewable industrial residues, and myce-

The “Living Cocoon” developed by Dutch researchers Bob Hendrikx and Lonneke 
Westhoff [78] represents a sustainable and eco-friendly alternative to traditional burial 
methods (Fig. 4) These coffins are made from mushroom mycelium, the vegetative part 
of fungi, which decomposes quickly and helps enrich the soil by neutralizing toxins and 
providing nutrients. The decomposition process in these coffins can take as little as 30 to 
45 days, significantly faster than traditional coffins, which can take years to break down. In 
Thailand, Thinniyom Co., Ltd. and BIOTEC-NSTDA of Thailand [79] have developed a pet 
coffin derived from spent mushroom substrate and corncob residues and is pre-order on sale 
in 2025 [80] (Supplemental Fig. 1). Mycelium possesses natural insulating properties that make it suitable for use in refrig-
erators, ovens, and other appliances. Traditional synthetic insulation materials made from 
polystyrene and polyurethane foams are highly flammable and non-sustainable [27]. Nev-
ertheless, for instance, insulation applications from MBC offer more significant advantages 
over synthetic foams and wood products and provide a major benefit in terms of fire safety 
compared to synthetic insulation foams. Cumulative forms of mycelium-based construc-
tion materials include block materials [81], building envelopes [82, 83], thermal insulations 
[84–87], particle board [23, 88, 89], and soundproofing mycelium/ acoustic materials [90, 
91] (Supplemental Fig. 2). The comparison MBCs with conventional construction materials highlights key advan-
tages and limitations. In terms of cost-effectiveness, MBCs are more affordable than materi-
als like steel, aluminum, and reinforced concrete, making them ideal for non-load-bearing 
applications such as interior panels and insulation. Regarding sustainability and recyclabil-
ity, MBCs, like straw bale, cob, and rammed earth, are fully biodegradable, offering high 
recyclability and aligning with circular economy principles. For mechanical performance, 
while MBCs have lower compressive strength than concrete, steel, and bamboo composites, 
their strength is comparable to eco-friendly materials such as straw bal

e, cork, and cob. In

Art, Art toy and Sculpture: Mycelium-based composites are increasingly popular in aesthetic 
interior design due to their unique texture. The surface of MBC products can range from 
smooth to rough, and their color can vary from off-white to brown, creating a natural and 
distinctive appearance [53]. These characteristics make MBC an attractive material for various 
design applications. Several categories of products have been explored, including a tabletop, 
a lampshade, and a bowl designed by Agata Bonenberg [53]. Various lampshade designs have 
been developed, such as Mycelium lights created in collaboration with Nir Meiri Studio and 
BIOHM, Pendant and B Wise lampshades from Myceen, and MushLume Lighting designed 
by Danielle Trofe and Grown.Bio [92–94], (Fig. 4). The Mycoschoen shoes, designed by artist 
Kristel Peterscourtesy, were showcased at the “Fungi Futures: Movements in Mycelium” exhi-
bition organized by Somerset House in London in 2020 (Fig. 4). MYC is a disposable barbecue 
grill made from edible fungus, corn cob, and bamboo sticks [95]. These examples highlight 
the broad applicability and aesthetic appeal of MBC in interior design and everyday products. To overcome the limitations of size and geometry in MBCs, 3D printing and digital 
fabrication of mycelium slurry have been explored as alternatives to traditional molding 
methods [72, 96, 97]. 3D printing of MBC can be achieved through liquid deposition mod-
eling (LDM), which requires the substrate to be in a gel form before printing. Therefore, 
3D-printed MBCs necessitate the inclusion of a gelling agent as an essential component. Currently, a variety of organic gelling agents, such as husk, corn starch, flour, xanthan gum, 
guar gum, paper cellulose, locust bean gum, and psyllium, are utilized in 3D printing for-
mulations to achieve the necessary substrate consistency [97]. Additionally, non-digestible 
components such as soil, sand, and clay are incorporated into these formulations [96, 98, 99]. Notable examples of 3D-printed MBC include the Mycelium Chair created by Eric Klar-
enbeek, which was printed with living fungus and displayed at Dutch Design Week 2013 
[100]. MyCera is a digitally printed clay-myce

lium composite where the mycelium grows 
within the clay structure, resulting in improved mechanical properties [98, 99], (Fig. 4). Additionally, Terreform ONE used Mycofoam to fabricate seating furniture, challenging 
traditional manufacturing methods [101]. A significant challenge in 3D printing MBC is the 
development of a sterile printing system. The printing nozzle, print head, and other compo-
nents must be sterilized prior to printing to prevent contamination. Packaging
Polystyrene, in its various forms, such as foams, films, and rigid plastics, is widely used 
in packaging across diverse industries, including food, electronics, and device protection. Expanded polystyrene is particularly favored for its low density, durability, and insulating 
properties, making it an ideal packaging material. However, styrene is a petroleum-derived 
product, which makes it an unsustainable resource for the packaging industry. In 2012, the 
Ecovative Design laboratory in Green Island, New York, embarked on research to develop 
pilot MBC foams. Their study revealed that the properties of MBC foams were comparable 
to those of polystyrene foams, offering a potentially sustainable alternative to traditional

polystyrene packaging [102]. Subsequently, the company established the Mushroom® Pack-
aging line [103], which includes MBC-based polystyrene replacements for wine shippers 
and gift boxes. GROWN bio, an MBC brand based in Germany and the Netherlands, pro-
duces mushroom-derived packaging for industrial applications, luxury items, and insula-
tion [104]. Dell produces mushroom-based cushions for packaging electronic devices [105]. Established in 2020, FungiTech (Malaysia), cultivates oyster mushrooms and converts farm 
waste into MBC for packing purposes [106]. Commercial MBC packaging materials are, to a certain degree, compostable. GROWN 
bio claims their product is 100% compostable, and Ecovative Design has asserted that their 
packaging materials are eco-friendly and can be composted quickly. These claims are sup-
ported by the observation that a reduction of up to 60% of the total mass was observed for 
MBC in the ISO 20,200 soil burial test [107]. Nevertheless, the biodegradability and bio-
compatibility of MBC packaging materials require further investigation. The thermo-mechanical properties of MBC foam packaging materials are comparable to 
expanded polystyrene foam and polyurethane foam. The tensile and compressive strengths 
of conventional expanded polystyrene are 7.2 and 4.8 MPa, respectively [108]. At this load-
ing, it may elongate up to 4.9% [108]. However, when heated to 80 °C, the tensile and com-
pressive strength values are reduced to 4.26 and 1.67 MPa, respectively [109]. Expanded 
polystyrene has a glass-transition temperature (Tg) of up to 390 °C, while polyurethane 
foams become amorphous at 240–255 °C [110]. The Tg of expanded polystyrene beads is 
about 350 °C [111]

. Ongoing research efforts are focused on further improving the proper-
ties of MBC foams. Recent studies have shown that MBC foams derived from sawdust and 
rice husk exhibited approximately twice the hardness of polystyrene foams [112]. Further-
more, another study demonstrated the potential of cellulose-mycelium foams (25-day myce-
lium growth), which exhibit a tensile strength up to 6 MPa (with approximately 35% strain) 
and a Tg of 326 °C [113]. The study revealed that the growth of mycelium may reduce the 
mechanical properties of cellulose but elevate its thermal performance [113]. When comparing the toxicity of packaging materials, burning 800 g of expanded poly-
styrene produces twice as much nitrous dioxide and 10 times more particulate matter than 
burning an equivalent amount of mycelium-sawdust MBC [114]. Nevertheless, the same 
MBC was also reported to produce two times more sulfur dioxide and carbon monoxide 
during the combustion [114]. These findings highlight the need for further research into the 
environmental impacts of MBC, as MBCs made from different carbon sources and myce-
lium species could result in different by-products during their combustion. Textiles/Fabrics/Paper-like and Fashion
Mycelium Leather: In addition to the ethical concerns of using animal products, the tanning 
process used to make animal skin leather requires chromium, which negatively impacts 
human health and the environment [115]. Leather manufactured from mycelium (mycelium 
leather) has properties similar to that of leather derived from animal skin and is a valid 
alternative material for the manufacturing of clothes and shoes. Mycelium leather can be 
produced in a more environment-friendly way than animal skin leather. Mycelium leather 
is fabricated by two main methods: liquid fermentation and solid-state fermentation [9]. Liquid fermentation often utilizes standard media or cost-effective agricultural by-products, 
such as blackstrap molasses, to promote fungal growth. The resultant biomass is then pro-

pressing, and drying to achieve the desired material properties [9, 116]. In the realms of 
industrial and construction materials, there has been a burgeoning interest in leveraging 
mycelium-based technologies [117]. Nonetheless, the scarcity of published experimental 
data, often because of proprietary knowledge or intellectual property concerns, poses sig-
nificant challenges to the advancement and application of innovative mycelium-based tech-

has been marketed as MuSkin by Grado Zero Innovation. Other examples of mycelium 
leather used in commercially available products include Sylvania leather (Hermes, in col-
laboration with Myco

Work, Fig. 4), and Mylo, manufactured by Bolt Threads [45]. Myce-
lium leather is available in various finishes such as pebble texture, natural mycelium looks, 
and smoothness. Later, Fine Mycelium was developed to control the direction of mycelium 
thread at the microscopic level, which has superior strength, durability, and performance, 
and can fit a wide range of applications [120]. Mylo leather collaborated with many brands, 
creating the concept Yoga Mat by Lululemon, Stan Smith Mylo by Adidas, Mylo Randoseru 
by Tsuchiya Kaban, and Mylo Wallet Bag by objcts.io [121]. Ecovative developed AirMy-
celium, a high-density foam-like pure mycelium, by controlling culture environments such 
as air flow, nutrients, space, and humidity. AirMycelium can be compressed into a leather-
like material, and its trade name is Forager [122]. Also, Forager™ developed its own verti-
cal farm to up-scale the production of Forager in the size of 60 × 4 m2 [123]. Mylea made by 
MYCL (Mycotech) is used in keychain, shirt, and wallet products [124]. These four majors’ 
companies published more than 150 patents, which cover mechanical property improve-
ment of mycelium, mycelium leather production system, and application of mycelium foam 
and leather [61, 125]. Apart from these established companies, several companies across 
the world are also focusing on the development of mycelium leather, for example, Bewider, 
Sporatex, NEFFA, Mylium, Mycofuture, Mycel, and Muush (Table 1). Clothing and Shoes: Sneature is a “bio-shoe” made from natural and renewable raw mate-
rials. The shoe is made from Chiengora, a protein-based 3D knit of natural rubber and myce-
lium designed to enhance stiffness, flexibility, and air circulation [126]. It also requires around 
20% less material in the production compared with a standard shoe. UYN URBAN TRAIL 
MUSHROOM MAN SHOES are also made with mycelium biomaterial protective shield 
layer [127]. Additionally, mycelium can be used as shoe sole because of its foam-like prop-
erty [128, 129]. Mycelium shoe soles have been developed with both MBC and PMM. PMM 
shoe soles were developed in a similar way as mycelium leather. In 2012, Ecovative produced 
AirMycelium™, a dense and thick mycelium sheet. This product is related to patents describ-
ing the technology of mycelium mat/sheet [130, 131]. MBC shoe soles have also been pro-
duced in several shoe designs. Espadrille shoe soles were made with king oyster mushrooms, 
chicken feathers, and nonwoven textiles to test consumer opinion [128]. From this survey, 
it was revealed that customers would likely purchase mycelium espadrilles due to their eco-
friendly and biodegradability features. A prototype sandal sole made using a combination of 
mushroom species and various agricultural wastes, mostly chicken feather and psyllium husk 
has also been reported [132, 133]. Mycelium heels made for the “Growing Shoes” project 
comprise solid and hollow models. While coffee grounds and sawdust were used

for the heel, 
platform, and insole, the hollow shoe shapes are grown pure in bottles or on fibers such as 
hemp and psyllium to achieve strength and structures [134]. The mushroom species used for

fire-resistant shoes fabricated from various agricultural wastes [139], and a mycelium helmet

[140] are examples of fire-resistant apparel that can be produced at a low cost. Paper: Paper manufacturing causes deforestation and other negative environmental impacts 
resulting from the use of toxic chemicals, such as kraft, sulfite, and sodium hydroxide, in the 
production process. Therefore, alternative sustainable natural polymer resources to tree bio-
polymer for paper manufacturing are desirable. The mycelium cell wall consists of chitin, chito-

plant fiber with mycelium began in the 1970s [141]. Various fungal species cultured in liquid 
media were filtered and mixed with unbleached aspen kraft pulp in a ratio of 0–50% [141]. However, only Saprolegnia ferax could enhance the tensile strength of the paper composite. Mycelium could be used as a 5–10% paper additive without appreciable reduction of tensile 
strength [142]. Since chitin is one of the main components in mycelium, it can be considered a 
chitin/chitosan resource. Even though the mechanical properties of extracted chitin nanofiber 
and nanocrystal are inferior to that of nanocellulose, various studies have agreed that mycelium 
can produce chitin at low cost [143–146]. Chitin nanofiber can be prepared via suspension, to 
form a fibrous sheet suitable for paper manufacturing [146]. While the preparation by solvent 
casting can lead to a non-porous sheet, impurities such as alcohol and acid derivatives increase 
the hydrophobicity of mycelium paper. The chitin nanofiber sheet from mycelium exhibits ten-
sile strength and moduli of more than 200 MPa and 7 GPa, respectively [143, 145]. Food Industry/and Beverage Containers

MBC Applications Under Research and Development
Environmental Remediation
In the field of MBC environmental remediation, a significant focus of ongoing research is 
on the removal of contaminants from aquatic ecosystems. An early study demonstrated the 
potential of dead fungal biomass for adsorbing textile dyes from solutions. Specifically, a 
suspension of Phanerochaete chrysosporium (20 g/L) was shown to remove nearly 80% 
of 100 mg/L Astrazone Blue dye within an hour, highlighting the effectiveness of fungal 
biomass in pollutant remediation efforts [168]. A biocomposite of dead biomass of Asper-
gillus niger coated with iron oxide was employed to remove arsenic from water [169], and 
A. niger mycelium biocomposites with alginate, ferric oxide, and dopamine were success-
fully employed for the adsorption of various chemical spec

ies of thorium [170]. Another 
study used Xylaria to create a fungal hyphae/graphene oxide aerogel biocomposite for the 
removal of uranium ions [U(VI)] [171]. This biocomposite adsorbed approximately 200 
mg/g of U(VI) in 30 min, with a maximum predicted adsorption capacity of 288.42 mg/g 
[171]. Furthermore, the adsorbent demonstrated selectivity for U(VI) even in the presence 
of other cations [171]. Another research showcased the development of alginate beads loaded with the fila-
mentous fungus Stereum hirsutum inside nanoporous silica hydrogels [172]. The adsorbent 
achieved an 80% removal of malachite green within 20 h at an initial pollutant concentra-
tion of 80 µM [172]. A mycelium composite as a chemical, gas, and odor absorber has 
been designed for sustainable diaper and cat litter applications [173]. A Shiitake mushroom 
diaper was created following a similar concept [174]. A phosphate-functionalized mycelium 
membrane sheet was also fabricated from the dried mycelium membranes of Ecovative 
Design [175]. This membrane was evaluated for Pb(II) removal using a continuous cross-
flow filtration system, achieving a removal efficiency of approximately 90% at a flux of 
1200 LMH with a 1500 mg/L Pb(II) solution [175]. For air pollution remediation, MBC made from Pleurotus ostreatus was investigated 
for the adsorption of NO₃⁻ and SO₄²⁻ from atmospheric particulate matter (PM) [176]. The 
results showed that using hemp as a substrate could remove 204 mg/kg of NO₃⁻, while a rice 
straw-based MBC could adsorb up to 1189 mg/kg of SO₄²⁻ [176]. The study suggested that 
for an MBC to adsorb PM effectively, a balance between water absorption (negative factor) 
and PM adsorption (positive factor) is crucial [176]. Research on soil remediation using MBCs is relatively limited, despite mycelium’s natu-
ral capacity to directly absorb contaminants such as heavy metals and toxins from the soil—
a process known as fungal phytoremediation [177]. Recent advancements in MBC research 
have facilitated the cultivation of mycelium on contaminated substrates to produce MBCs. Depending on the nature of the contamination and the fungal species employed, the toxins 
within the substrate can be broken down by the fungus, sequestered within the mycelium, or 
are accumulated in the fruiting bodies, which are then harvested for further treatment [178]. In each scenario, the myceliated substrate has the potential to be transformed into composite 
materials, provided that their toxicity levels are thoroughly assessed. 1 3

Agriculture, Animal-Pet, and Horticulture
Mycelium can serve as a medium in aquaponics systems to enhance sustainable food pro-
duction. Mycelial networks efficiently filter water and improve the overall health of the 
aquaponics ecosystem by facilitating nutrient absorption and providing a habitat for ben-
eficial microorganisms. A notable example is the work of Rhode Island School of De

sign 
students and researchers, who have created floating planting beds made of mycelium bio-
material to cleanse waterways of pollutants and restore wetland habitats. These floating 
Biopods return native plants to degraded wetland systems while cleansing the water through 
bioremediation, utilizing microorganisms that naturally decontaminate their environment. Furthermore, the biodegradable nature of mycelium ensures that the system remains envi-
ronmentally friendly [179]. In addition, MBCs provide a sustainable alternative for manufacturing biodegradable 
gardening products such as planters, pots, and gardening tools. These mycelium-derived 
products are robust, lightweight, and naturally decompose over time. The use of these prod-
ucts reduces plastic waste and contributes to more sustainable gardening practices, minimiz-
ing environmental impact while enhancing plant growth conditions by providing a natural, 
nutrient-rich environment [104, 180, 181]. Moreover, mycelium-based planting blocks rep-
resent an innovative application in horticulture, offering a sustainable medium for plant 
cultivation. These blocks can be infused with nutrients and beneficial microorganisms to 
support plant growth. As plants outgrow the blocks, the mycelium decomposes, enriching the soil with organic 
matter and improving soil health. This closed-loop system exemplifies sustainable horti-
cultural practices, reducing the reliance on synthetic growing media [182]. MBCs are also 
well-suited for constructing beehives and pet cages, offering a natural and sustainable 
alternative to conventional materials. These structures leverage the insulating properties of 
mycelium, which helps to shelter inhabitants from extreme weather conditions. A key area 
of research within the HIVEOPOLIS project focuses on the modulation and sustainable 
integration of myco-materials into biohybrid beehive designs. This innovative approach 
seeks to enhance honeybee habitats by combining biological elements (such as fungal net-
works and bees) with artificial intelligence components (including sensors, actuators, and 
dancing robot filtering), ultimately improving the functionality and sustainability of future 
beehives [183, 184]. Health and Medical Devices and Supplies
Mycelium materials have been proposed as a tissue regenerative matrix for use in medical 
applications due to their unique structural properties (Fig. 5). Mycelium consists of a net-
work of branching fibers that form the foundation of mushrooms or fungi, characterized by a 
microporous network. The cell wall of mycelium is composed of polysaccharides, β-glucan, 
and chitin, which confer significant advantages for wound healing applications by promot-
ing epithelial regeneration and enhancing immune response efficiency [185, 186]. β-glucans 
can bind to receptors on immune cells such as Dectin-1, resulting in the production of cyto-
kines and chemokines like TNF-α, IL-6, IL-12, and GM-CSF, which are crucial for immune

response and inflammation [187–189]. Additionally, β-glucans within mycelium play a cru-
cial role in promoting the maturation of macrophages, thereby enhancing their capacity

keratinocytes. This is vital for re-epithelialization, as it influences the expression of growth 
factors such as VEGF (vascular endothelial growth factor) and TGF-β (transforming growth 
factor-beta), both of which are essential for tissue regeneration and repair. Furthermore, gly-
cosaminoglycans (GAGs) present in the outer layer of the mycelium cell wall can bind with 
key proteins during adhesion processes, including growth factors, cytokines, and related 
proteins within the extracellular matrix. These properties highlight the high potential of mycelium for use in fabricating scaffolds 
for regenerative applications (Fig. 5). The gradient porous structure of mycelium closely 
mimics the mammalian extracellular matrix, which facilitates cell adhesion, proliferation, 
and the efficient transfer of nutrients and waste during cell growth, further underscoring its 
suitability for regenerative medicine [190]. Furthermore, myco-biomaterials show promise 
as smart devices for skin wound healing applications [186]. Several studies have employed chemical treatments to improve the mechanical properties 
of mycelium fibers for medical applications. For example, applying plasticizers to increase 
fiber flexibility or using chemical crosslinking techniques, such as inducing COO- func-
tional groups via TEMPO oxidation and crosslinking with Ca2+, has demonstrated shape-
recovery materials [191–193]. Moreover, 3D printing was employed to fabricate mycelium 
hydrogel [194, 195], which was proven to influence colonization of the gel and the bridging 
of air gaps, thus affecting the cell growth. A mycelium composite marketed by Ecovative has been explored for its potential as a 
prosthesis socket [196], which was identified based its properties of low specific gravity, 
low surface hardness, and acceptable thermal expansion. However, a significant drawback 
of mycelium composites is their extremely high-water absorption. To mitigate this issue, 
a hydrophobic coating would be necessary for practical application in prosthesis socket. Additionally, there is potential to develop an alternative single-layer mask that utilizes fun-
gal mycelium as self-growing filaments, which could enhance the efficiency of individual 
polypropylene mask layers [197]. Amulet, Religious, and Traditional Products

inspiration 
from themes of death, grief, decay, and regeneration, the artist delves into the cycles of life

internship at Mississippi Mushrooms have further honed this multidisciplinary practice. The 
sculpture series debuted at Franconia Sculpture Park in 2021, where the pieces biodegraded

accessed on March 07, 2024) is an artwork meticulously crafted from cultivated lingzhi 
mushrooms, mycelium, and wood chips, exploring themes of transformation, growth, and 
the intersection of nature and culture. By incorporating lingzhi mushrooms, revered for 
their medicinal properties and cultural significance in Chinese tradition, alongside living 
mycelium, Yan creates a dynamic sculpture that blurs the boundaries between life and

widely exhibited piece underscores the interconnectedness of life and emphasizes the poten-

offerings to deities, spirits, or ancestors. Spirits are integral to daily life and are seen as con-
duits to prosperity or sources of protection. According to Thai beliefs, when a spirit grants 
a wish, the individual must return to the shrine and present an offering. Over the years, 
zebras have become popular offerings at shrines and spirit houses nationwide. To address 
the visual and air pollution caused by traditional votive animal figures, BIOTEC-NSTDA 
and the Forest Friends Project, in collaboration with Dentsu Creative Thailand, developed 
an innovative solution called “Zero Pollution Zebra” using agri

cultural waste and mycelium 
as the binding agent (Fig. 5). This initiative aims to reduce the environmental impact of 
these offerings, providing a more sustainable and eco-friendlier alternative [200]. Through-
out history, divine sculptures have been made from the finest materials of their time, such 
as the gold-plated Buddha at Wat Pho and Michelangelo’s Pietà. Furf Design Studio rein-
terprets these historic statues with modern materials and techniques, combining biomaterial 
mycelium with 3D printing technology. This innovative method enables the creation of sustainable deities by molding mycelium 
in 3D-printed forms, resulting in eco-friendly sculptures that blend traditional artistic inspi-
ration with contemporary sustainability practices [201]. The mushroom coffin, produced by 
the Dutch company The Loop and aptly named “The Living Coffin,” symbolizes the final link 
in the Circle of Life, where all organisms die, decompose, and return to the natural elemen-
tal world (Fig. 4). In this process, other living organisms consume the chemical and nutrient 
remains of the decomposed body, completing the cycle [202]. Lastly, the Mushroom Death 
Suit, designed by Jae Rhim Lee and produced by Coeio, is an eco-friendly burial garment 
crafted from organic cotton and embroidered with thread infused with mushroom spores. As 
the body decomposes, the spores are activated, facilitating the breakdown of tissues and the 
neutralization of environmental contaminants such as heavy metals and pharmaceuticals. This sustainable alternative to traditional burial methods significantly reduces the ecologi-
cal footprint by eliminating the need for harmful embalming fluids and non-biodegradable 
caskets. The suit promotes a harmonious return to nature, challenging conventional burial 
practices and encouraging environmentally responsible end-of-life choices [202]. 1 3

Space and Automotive Industries
In 2020, NASA’s Ames Research Center pioneered an innovative approach to space habi-
tation through its myco-architecture project [203]. This initiative has been exploring the 
potential of fungi to construct living quarters for future lunar and Martian expeditions. The 
concept envisions a compact, MBC structure that astronauts could transport and cultivate 
upon arrival. The proposed habitat features a tri-layer design: an outer shell of ice for radia-
tion shielding, a middle layer of cyanobacteria for oxygen generation, and an inner layer 
of mycelium that forms the actual living space. This early-stage research, supported by 
NASA’s Innovative Advanced Concepts program, aims not only to reduce the energy costs 
associated with traditional habitat designs but also to explore additional applications such 
as water purification (Fig. 5), biomining, and self-healing structures. Brandić Lipińska et al. [204, 205] proposed using MBCs to “grow” extraterrestrial structures on-site, reducing the 
need for tr

ansporting heavy materials and minimizing robotic operations. Due to their resemblance to Earth’s natural elements, MBC offers various suitable prop-
erties such as self-replication, self-healing, sensing capabilities, waste processing, radiation 
protection, and psychological benefits. Under the mWALLd concept, Pawlicki and Bałka 
[206] explored the use of MBC for constructing habitats in space, particularly on Mars. The idea emphasized methods like mold filling and 3D printing with inoculated feedstock 
to form large, irregular structures without the need for pre-made molds. Additionally, Hanna 
et al. [207] evaluated the potential of in-space manufacturing of fungal biocomposite mate-
rial through preliminary experiments. Their research revealed that Schizophyllum commune 
can grow on Azolla filiculoides (AF) substrate under microgravity conditions, surviving for 
three days even at a gamma radiation dose level of 200 Gy. They produced small-scale AF/
SC biocomposite panels for mechanical testing at the European Space Research and Tech-
nology Centre. They also developed tools for extrusion and 3D printing of mycelium-based 
pastes, creating variously shaped 3D-printed mycelium composite objects. MBCs offer potential advantages in terms of sustainability, weight reduction, and ver-
satility for space and automotive industries. However, further research and development 
are needed to overcome challenges such as scalability, durability in extreme conditions, 
and meeting industry standards. As technology advances, we may see increasing adoption 
of these materials in space and automotive applications. For example, Antolin, a global 
provider of technological solutions for car interiors, has developed a pioneering project 
that employs mycelium-based natural materials to help reduce the environmental impact of 
vehicle components [208]. Electronic and Informatic Device
Electronic waste, an increasing issue associated with our use of electronic devices, demands 
careful management. When these devices are no longer practically useful, they are often dis-
carded, potentially leading to environmental contamination. These discarded devices con-
tain valuable metals like gold and copper, but they also harbor hazardous substances such 
as lead and mercury (Fig. 5). Additionally, the manufacturing processes of conventional 
electronic devices and their consumables contribute to a continuous waste stream. In the realm of electronics, MBCs and similar materials have been extensively studied as 
potential components of sensor devices. For instance, a study reported the electrical activity

of a slime mold placed in two blobs of agar, connected by a protoplasmic tube. This setup, 
within a controlled Petri dish environment, involved the use of aluminum electrodes to 
monitor the slime mold’s electrical activity, demonstrating the potential of biological mate-
rials in electronic applications [2

09]. The system was used as a color sensor to differenti-
ate red, green, white, and blue light [209]. Under the same concept of the changing power 
of carbohydrates, the setup was also successfully utilized for sensing different chemicals 
[210]. To some degree, signal transmission was also achievable with two blocks of living 
MBC connected by a platinum wire [211]. In addition, MBC materials have also been used 
as weight and pressure sensors [212, 213]. Another electronic application of MBC is their use as components of circuit boards with 
embedded electronic devices [214]. Danninger et al. [215] introduced the concept of Myc-
elioTronics, which utilizes MBC to create flexible and biodegradable electronic mycelium 
skins. The study also showcased MBC-based batteries, which powered a sensor board to 
measure and transmit data. In the field of computation, it was found that living MBC mate-
rials could function as logical circuits as they transformed electrical signals non-linearly 
and handled complex Boolean operations [216]. However, the constant growth of living 
mycelium in composites caused variability in results [216]. From sensors and circuit boards 
to batteries and computational systems, MBC demonstrates versatility across various elec-
tronic applications. The ability to create biodegradable and flexible electronic components 
offers a potential pathway to reduce the environmental impact of electronic waste. While 
challenges remain, particularly in avoiding living mycelium for consistent performance, the 
progress in MBC technology represents a significant step towards more sustainable elec-
tronics and informatics. Future of MBMs and PMMs
The future of MBMs and PMMs lies in their potential to revolutionize industries through 
sustainable innovation, customization, and multifunctionality [217]. These advancements 
offer significant opportunities to address critical environmental, social, and economic chal-
lenges while driving the transition to a bio-based and circular economy [18, 88]. Adaptive and Multifunctional Materials
The development of MBMs and PMMs is shifting toward creating adaptive and multifunc-
tional materials that can respond to external stimuli, such as temperature, humidity, and 
mechanical stress. Advanced fabrication techniques, such as integrating responsive poly-
mers or functional coatings, could enable self-healing, shape-memory, or thermally adaptive 
properties. For instance, adaptive bricks or panels for construction could adjust to environ-
mental changes, enhancing energy efficiency and durability in buildings. Currently, most 
MBMs are designed for a single specific purpose. However, future developments are poised 
to transcend these limitations by focusing on multifunctionality and adaptability. By lever-
aging the inherent structural complexity of mycelium, these materials can be combined with 
advanced polymers or engineered coatings to develop self-repairing and shape-recovering 
capabilities [125,

218, 219]. Such innovations would allow MBMs to actively respond to 
various external factors, making them ideal for dynamic applications, including self-healing

Circular Economy and Sustainability (2025) 5:3211–3253
bricks or adaptive concrete for construction [220]. These advancements not only enhance 
material performance but also support long-term sustainability by reducing maintenance 
and extending the lifespan of products. Advanced Applications in Electronics and Sensory Devices
Mycelium fibers, with their intricate network structure, hold the potential to serve as com-
munication pathways for sensors, functioning in a manner akin to neural networks. To 
fully harness this capability, further exploration of the electrical and optical properties of 
mycelium-based materials is essential, paving the way for a wide range of applications. For 
instance, intelligent sensing insoles have been developed as an initial step toward creat-
ing more generalized reactive fungal wearables. These insoles demonstrate how excitation 
wavefronts within a mycelium network can detect pressure points through electrical activity 
[213]. Furthermore, MBCs can be integrated into electrical circuits with nonlinear electrical 
properties, including applications such as printed circuit boards [221] and mycelium-based 
skins for sustainable electronics [215, 222]. Multi-Organism Materials/Engineered Living Composites
The development of multi-organism materials, combining mycelium with other biologi-
cal organisms, is an emerging area aimed at supporting global sustainability goals. This 
includes integrating mycelium with seaweeds, bacterial or microbial cellulose, and plants, 
leveraging their cellulose, chitin, and protein structures to create biopolymer-based materi-
als [14, 223, 224]. For instance, mycelium and bacterial cellulose can co-exist and form 
composites with diverse functional properties [225–227]. Examples include the creation 
of jute-mycelium-based vegan leather using hessian jute fabric, natural rubber, and poly-
hydroxyalkanoate biopolymer extracted from Bacillus subtilis [228], and composites made 
from wheat straws and polypropylene embedded with spores of B. amyloliquefaciens [229]. Myco-Remediation and Environmental Applications
Further research into mycelium networks and their composition is essential for optimizing 
their use as effective absorbers in air pollution control, such as microbial aerosol filtra-
tion [230], and in water treatment applications, where fungal pellets have shown poten-
tial for biosorbing heavy metals, dyes, phenolic compounds, humic substances, pesticides, 
and pharmaceuticals [231, 232]. Mycelium’s role in pollutant removal includes innovative 
approaches like the immobilization of fungal mycelia and their enzymes, such as laccase 
from Trametes hirsuta in alginate-cellulose beads for dye decolorization [233], and the use 
of Pleurotus fossulatus for nanoparticle remediation in contaminated water, demo

nstrating 
its potential in industrial wastewater treatment [234]. Additionally, mycelium-based pellets 
have been effectively employed in the removal of organochlorine compounds [235], and 
the synergistic degradation of kerosene by non-cultivable bacterial and fungal mycelium 
offers another promising avenue for environmental remediation [236]. Innovations such 
as mycelium-based filtration membranes, bio-absorbent panels, and pollutant-sequestering 
composites could address critical environmental issues like microplastic contamination and 
toxic spills. 1 3
3236

Circular Economy and Sustainability (2025) 5:3211–3253
Thread-liked Mycelium for Woven Textile
The development of thread-like mycelium for woven textiles represents a groundbreak-
ing advancement in sustainable material science with the potential to revolutionize the 
textile industry. Mycelium, with its natural hyphal network, offers a renewable and 
eco-friendly alternative to traditional fibers. While its current applications are mostly 
limited to nonwoven sheets as vegan leather substitutes, advances in cultivation and 
processing are paving the way for its use in thread-like structures. By optimizing 
growth conditions and integrating mycelium with natural polymers like bacterial cel-
lulose, researchers can create threads with enhanced strength, flexibility, and durability, 
suitable for large-scale production. Once harvested, mycelium threads can undergo mechanical or chemical treatments to 
improve their properties and be blended with natural fibers such as cotton or jute. These 
composite textiles hold promise for applications across eco-friendly fashion, medical tex-
tiles, and industrial fabrics. Mycelium’s low environmental impact, biodegradability, and 
capacity to utilize agricultural waste make it a key material for sustainable textiles. Compa-
nies like MycoWorks, which has advanced control over mycelium fiber alignment in prod-
ucts like FineMycelium™, exemplify the potential of this innovation to redefine the fabric 
industry. The evolution of true mycelium threads could provide a high-performance, renew-
able alternative to synthetic fibers, addressing the urgent need for sustainable solutions in 
global textile production [45, 237]. Sustainable Food (mycoprotein) and Nutraceutical Production
Mycelium’s rapid growth and nutrient content make it a promising candidate for sustainable 
food production. Future research could explore its potential in producing high-quality pro-
tein, functional foods, and nutraceuticals. Mycelium-based meat alternatives and fermented 
food products could address food security challenges while reducing the environmental 
impact of traditional agriculture. Additionally, mycelium’s bioactive compounds could be 
harnessed for developing health-promoting supplements and medicines Mycelium, known 
for its ease of cultivation and rapid growth, is rich in essential nutrients such as carbohy-
drates and proteins, making it a promising candidate

for future food production. Its potential for mass production, whether in industrial settings or through in-house 
cultivation, positions mycoprotein as a novel, sustainable food ingredient with significant 
global market growth potential [12, 238]. Beyond technological advancements, mycelium 
is critical for developing food products that are not only nutritious and desirable but also 
scalable, affordable, and environmentally sustainable. It offers a scalable biomass solution 
for producing food with high-quality protein, fiber, and essential micronutrients, all crucial 
for improving public health. Given its environmental sustainability, mycelium-based food 
products are particularly well-suited to address issues of malnutrition and undernutrition, 
which are often exacerbated by food insecurity and diets lacking in macro- and micronutri-
ent density [57, 239–241]. 1 3
3237

Circular Economy and Sustainability (2025) 5:3211–3253
Space and Extreme Environments
Mycelium’s lightweight, self-replicating, and insulating properties make it an attractive 
candidate for extraterrestrial applications. In space exploration, MBMs could be used to 
construct habitats, radiation shields, or life-support systems on Mars or the Moon. NASA’s 
research into mycelium-based habitats demonstrates the feasibility of creating structures 
on-site using local resources, reducing the logistical challenges of transporting building 
materials [8, 9]. Similarly, mycelium-based materials could be adapted for use in extreme 
Earth environments, such as polar regions or deserts, where conventional materials are less 
effective. Customized Mycelium Products
Advancements in biotechnology and materials science will enable the customization of 
MBMs and PMMs for specific applications. By tailoring growth conditions, substrate com-
positions, and fungal species, manufacturers can optimize properties such as strength, flex-
ibility, thermal insulation, and water resistance. Customized products, from biodegradable 
packaging to high-performance insulation, will cater to diverse industries, including auto-
motive, construction, and consumer goods. Integration With Circular Economy Principles
As industries adopt circular economy models, MBMs and PMMs are uniquely positioned to 
play a central role. Their ability to upcycle agricultural waste into valuable products aligns 
with sustainable production practices. Future developments could focus on creating closed-
loop systems where waste streams from one industry serve as raw materials for MBM pro-
duction, enhancing resource efficiency and reducing waste. Scaling and Commercialization
A critical challenge for the future of MBMs and PMMs is scaling up production to meet 
industrial demands. Innovations in vertical farming, automated cultivation systems, and 
large-scale bioreactors could improve scalability while maintaining product quality. Addi-
tionally, investments in research and development, as well as partnerships between aca-
d

emia and industry, will be essential to overcoming technical and economic barriers to 
commercialization [14]. Consumer Education and Market Acceptance
For MBMs and PMMs to gain widespread adoption, consumer education and market accep-
tance are crucial. Awareness campaigns highlighting the environmental and functional ben-
efits of these materials could drive demand. Collaborations with designers, architects, and 
product developers will showcase the versatility of mycelium-based materials, inspiring 
innovative applications in sustainable fashion, furniture, and lifestyle products [15]. 1 3
3238

Circular Economy and Sustainability (2025) 5:3211–3253
Challenges and Limitations in Current Synthesis Methods for 
Mycelium-Based Breakthroughs
Despite the growing interest in MBCs and other biocomposites as sustainable alternatives to 
conventional materials, several challenges persist in their synthesis methods, particularly in 
terms of time efficiency and scalability. These limitations hinder broader industrial adoption 
and commercial viability. Extended Cultivation and Processing time
The natural growth cycle of fungal mycelium poses a significant challenge in terms of pro-
duction speed. Unlike synthetic materials that can be manufactured in hours or days, MBCs 
and PMMs typically require several days to weeks for sufficient colonization and material 
formation. The duration depends on factors such as fungal species, substrate composition, 
and environmental conditions. Additionally, post-growth processing steps, including dry-
ing, heat treatment, and surface finishing, further extend the total production timeline. Scalability Constraints in Controlled Growth Conditions
Large-scale production of MBCs and PMMs requires precise control of environmental 
factors such as temperature, humidity, and oxygen levels. Maintaining optimal growth 
conditions across industrial-scale production facilities is technically complex and energy-
intensive. Inconsistent environmental conditions can lead to variations in material density, 
strength, and overall quality, posing a challenge for standardization. Heterogeneous Material Properties
The inherent biological variability of fungal growth can result in inconsistencies in mechan-
ical performance, density, and durability. Unlike traditional polymer-based composites, 
which are engineered with precise formulations, MBCs and PMMs are subject to natural 
variations influenced by substrate type, moisture content, and fungal metabolism. This vari-
ability makes it difficult to achieve uniform mechanical properties across large production 
batches. Moisture Sensitivity and Durability Concerns
MBCs and PMMs are inherently hygroscopic, meaning they absorb moisture from the 
environment, which can lead to structural degradation over time. While heat or chemical 
treatments can improve water resistance, these processes add additional production steps, 
increase costs, and may introduce sustainability concerns related to

chemical use. Post-Processing and Finishing Challenges
Unlike synthetic composites that can be easily molded or machined, MBCs and PMMs 
often require additional surface treatments, reinforcement strategies, or composite layer-
ing to meet durability and strength requirements for specific applications. These additional 
1 3
3239

Circular Economy and Sustainability (2025) 5:3211–3253
processing steps increase labor costs and production time, further complicating large-scale 
manufacturing. Limited Structural Strength Compared To Conventional Materials
While MBCs and PMMs have demonstrated promising mechanical properties, their com-
pressive and tensile strengths remain lower than those of conventional construction materi-
als like concrete, plastics, or engineered wood. Without additional reinforcements, such as 
fiber additives or hybrid material integration, their load-bearing capacity remains a limiting 
factor for structural applications. Energy and Resource Efficiency in Drying and Curing Processes
The drying and curing stages of MBC and PMMs production are critical for stabilizing the 
material and preventing unwanted microbial growth. However, these processes often require 
significant energy input, particularly for heat treatment methods. Achieving energy-efficient 
drying techniques that do not compromise material integrity remains an ongoing challenge. Regulatory and Standardization Barriers
Due to the relatively recent emergence of MBCs in industrial applications, standardized 
testing protocols and regulatory approvals remain limited. Industries such as construction, 
automotive, and packaging require materials to meet stringent safety, fire resistance, and 
durability standards. The lack of universally recognized benchmarks for MBCs slows down 
their adoption in commercial markets. To overcome these challenges (6.1–6.8), research efforts are focusing on optimizing 
fungal growth kinetics, engineering faster-growing fungal strains, and improving substrate 
formulations for enhanced colonization efficiency. Automation and bioreactor-based pro-
duction systems may help scale up MBC and PMMs synthesis while ensuring environ-
mental consistency. Furthermore, advancements in hybrid biocomposites—where MBCs 
are reinforced with natural fibers, nanocellulose, or biodegradable polymers—may enhance 
mechanical properties and broaden their applicability. Addressing these limitations is essen-
tial for unlocking the full potential of MBCs and biocomposites in sustainable material 
applications. As research progresses, further innovations in bioprocessing, material engi-
neering, and biofabrication will likely drive the transition toward more scalable, efficient, 
and commercially viable production methods. Conclusion
This review highlights the transformative potential of MBCs and PMMs in sustainable 
materials engineering. By utilizing agricultural residues and fungal mycelium, these biode-
gradable materials offer a viable alternative to synth

etic options. In-depth analysis of recent 
patent landscaping and academic publications were revealed. Over 40 companies across five 
continents involved in the commercialization of mycelium-based products were systemati-
cally analyzed. Eleven key application areas have been identified, spanning commercialized 
1 3
3240

Circular Economy and Sustainability (2025) 5:3211–3253
products and ongoing research in environmental remediation, healthcare, agriculture, cul-
tural artifacts, space exploration, and electronics. These innovations address sustainability 
challenges while advancing multifunctional materials with self-healing, shape-memory, and 
thermally adaptive properties. Integrating advanced fabrication techniques with mycelium’s 
adaptability redefines material science, enabling modular designs and multi-functional 
production. Prefabricated mycelium panels provide sustainable building solutions, while 
hybrid facilities and decentralized micro-factories enhance efficiency, reducing waste and 
emissions. Automation, AI-assisted cultivation, and circular manufacturing further optimize 
production. Future research should focus on exploring novel fungal species, refining substrate formu-
lations, assessing biodegradability, and improving scalability through bioreactor cultivation 
and AI-driven optimization. Expanding applications in healthcare, such as mycelium-based 
medical scaffolds and biocompatible prosthetics, could open new frontiers in biotechnology 
and regenerative medicine. Strengthening industry adoption will require standardization 
and certification to facilitate market integration and ensure consumer confidence. The adop-
tion of MBCs and PMMs in manufacturing reduces reliance on petroleum-based materials 
while supporting circular economy principles. Growing consumer awareness and regula-
tory backing will drive market expansion, necessitating clear industry standards and certi-
fications. Establishing interdisciplinary multisectoral collaborations between researchers, 
industries, and policymakers will accelerate the transition of MBCs from niche applications 
to widespread commercial adoption. Overall, fungal biotechnology is reshaping sustainable material development, offering 
scalable, cost-effective, and environmentally responsible solutions. As MBCs and PMMs 
evolve, they play a crucial role in advancing a circular bioeconomy, aligning with global 
sustainability goals. Continued interdisciplinary multisectoral research and industry col-
laboration are essential to unlocking their full potential, driving material innovation for a 
more resilient and eco-friendly future. Supplementary Information  The online version contains supplementary material available at ​h​t​t​p​s​:​/​/​d​o​i​.​o​r​g​
